---
title: Computational Methods to Reduce Bias in Binary Endpoints in Dose-finding Studies
title_short: Computational Methods to Reduce Bias in Binary Endpoints in Dose-finding Studies
subtitle: "TAC Meeting No. 1"
institute: "Institute of Medical Biometry \\break University of Heidelberg"
author: "Alexandra Balzer"
author_short: "Alexandra Balzer"
date: "2026-02-05"
date-format: "DD.MM.YYYY"
format: 
  beamer:
    template: markdown_beamer_template.tex
    keep-tex: true
    cite-method: natbib
linestretch: 1.1
header-includes:
    - \input{imbi_layout_markdownbeamer_en.tex}
bibliography: bibliography.bib
biblio-title: "References"
classoption: "aspectratio=169"
# Uncomment the following to two lines and insert the output path
# knit: (function(inputFile, encoding) {
#  rmarkdown::render(inputFile, encoding = "UTF-8", output_dir = "Put/output/path/here",  output_file = paste0("STUDYTITLE_report_", Sys.Date()) )})
---

```{r setup, include=FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(
    echo = FALSE
)
```

```{r dependencies, include=FALSE}
library(tidyverse)
library(gridExtra)
library(kableExtra)
library(gt)
```

### Content
\tableofcontents

# Introduction

### Introduction


* Optimization of dose finding and selection is a key goal in oncology drug research and development.
* OPTIMUS Project: FDA initiative to improve dose optimization/dose finding.
* Key challenge: Accurate estimation of therapeutic effects
  * True treatment effect is often overestimated in Phase II
  * Phase III results may not confirm Phase II findings.
* Current: Bias-correction methods were presented for normally distributed outcomes ([@tianyu2024])
* No methods exist yet for binary endpoints as tumor response.

# Previous Work

---

\textcolor{uniblue}{\Huge \textbf{Previous Work}}

---

### Proposed Methods for normally distributed endpoints

In [@tianyu2024] several methods are compared for reducing bias in normally distributed endpoints:

* Jackknife,
* **Single, Double and Triple (Non-) Parametric Bootstrap**,
* Shrinkage Estimator by Hwang  [@Hwang] and Lindley [@Lindey],
* Hybrid estimators. 

### Setup

* $I$ active treatment arms with $n$ patients.
* Response $X_{ij}$ for patient $j$ in group $i$ with $X_{ij} \sim g(\theta_i)$ and $i = 1, \dots, I$. 
* $\mathbf{X_i} = (X_{i1}, \dots, X_{in})$ vector of responses for group $i$ and $\mathbf{X} = (\mathbf{X_1}, \dots, \mathbf{X_I}).$


* Parameter of interest is $\theta_{max} = \max(\theta_1, \dots, \theta_I).$
* Estimator:  $\hat{\theta}(\mathbf{X}) = \max[\bar{\mathbf{X}}_1, \dots ,\bar{\mathbf{X}}_I]$.
* Even if $\bar{\mathbf{X}}_i = \mathrm{mean}(X_{i1}, \dots, X_{in})$ is an unbiased estimator, $\hat{\theta}(\mathbf{X})$ is biased.


### Idea of the Bootstrap Method

* The bias of the initial estimator is: 
$$\mathrm{Bias} = \mathrm{E}[\hat{\theta}(\mathbf{X})] - \theta_{max}.$$
* Bootstrap can be used to estimate this bias: 
$$\widehat{\mathrm{Bias}} = \hat{\mathrm{E}}[\hat{\theta}(\mathbf{X_B})] - \hat{\theta}(\mathbf{X}).$$
* This yields a bias-reduced estimator:
$$\hat{\theta}^{(1)}(\mathbf{X}) = \hat{\theta}(\mathbf{X}) - \widehat{\mathrm{Bias}}.$$


# My Simulation Study

---

\textcolor{uniblue}{\Huge \textbf{My Simulation Study}}

---

### General Setup
::: columns
::: column

* The simulation study consists of three parts.
* For each part, 10.000 repetitions are performed.
* The study design includes nine dose-response relationships each with 5 doses [@Ivanova2012].
* For each dose level, $n = 40$ patients per arm are considered.
* Binary endpoints are examined (e.g., response).

:::

::: column

```{r 1, echo = F, out.width = "100%"}

knitr::include_graphics("U:/Thesis/programs/Simulation final/Dose-Response.jpg")

```
:::
:::

## Part I - Optimization of Bootstrap Repetitions

### Part I - Optimization of Bootstrap Repetitions
**Aim**: Optimize the number of bootstrap repetitions $B$ for the single and double (non-)parametric bootstrap in binary endpoints, based on bias and MSE.

* Triple bootstrap is not considered in this part due to computational burden.
* The number of bootstrap repetitions $B$ is set to 80, 100, 200, 500, and 1000.

## Part II - Bayesian Hierarchical Models
### Part II - Bayesian Hierarchical Models

**Aim**: Identify the optimal hyperprior (with corresponding parameters) for a Bayesian hierarchical model to minimize bias and MSE.

The model is specified as: $Y_i \sim \mathrm{Bin}(n, p_i), \quad i = 1, \dots, 5$ with

$$ \mathrm{logit}(p_i) \sim \mathcal{N}(\mu, \sigma^2), \quad \mu \sim \mathcal{N}(-1.38, 100).$$

* Hyperpriors determine how much information is pooled across groups (i.e., $\sigma^2$ controls degree of shrinkage).

* The following MCMC settings are applied fo all fits: burn-in 2.000, number of chains 3, total MCMC iterations per chain: 10.000.

### Part II - Priors

The priors and corresponding parameters to evaluate are based on [@Cunanan]:

* $\mathrm{Unif}[0, a]$ for $\log(\sigma), \sigma, \sigma^2$ with
  * $a \in \{1, 2, 5, 10, 25, 50, 100, 500, 1000\}$.

* Half-t for $\sigma$ with
  * scale parameter $s \in \{0.5, 1, 2.5, 10, 25, 50, 100, 250, 500, 1000\}$ and
  * degrees of freedom $df \in \{1, 2, 5, 10, 25, 50, 100, 500\}$.

* Gamma distribution $\Gamma(a,b)$ for $\tau = \frac{1}{\sigma^2}$ with
  * $a \in \{0.005, 0.05, 0.25, 0.5, 1, 2, 5\}$, and
  * $b \in  \{0.0005, 0.001, 0.005, 0.01, 0.1, 0.5, 1, 2, 5, 20, 50\}$.



# Results

---

\textcolor{uniblue}{\Huge \textbf{Results}}

---
### Part I

\pause

```{r 2, echo = F, out.width = "54%", fig.align = 'left'}

knitr::include_graphics("U:/Thesis/programs/Simulation final/Bias_Bootstrap.png")

```

### Part I


```{r 2.1, echo = F, out.width = "100%"}

knitr::include_graphics("U:/Thesis/programs/Simulation final/Part I.png")

```

### Part I: Summary

* No relevant differences in bias or MSE across bootstrap iterations.
* $B = 80$ bootstrap iterations provide adequate bias correction.


### Part II: Uniform

\pause

```{r 4, echo = F, out.width = "56%", fig.align='left'}

knitr::include_graphics("U:/Thesis/programs/Simulation final/bias_uniform.png")

```

### Part II: Uniform

```{r 4.1, echo = F, out.width = "100%"}

knitr::include_graphics("U:/Thesis/programs/Simulation final/Uniform.png")

```


### Part II: Half-t

\pause

```{r 5, echo = F, fig.align = 'left', out.width = "55%"}

knitr::include_graphics("U:/Thesis/programs/Simulation final/bias_halft.png")

```

### Part II: Half-t



```{r 5.1, echo = F, out.width = "100%"}

knitr::include_graphics("U:/Thesis/programs/Simulation final/half-t.png")

```

### Part II: Gamma

\pause

```{r 6, echo = F, out.width = "55%", fig.align= 'left'}

knitr::include_graphics("U:/Thesis/programs/Simulation final/bias_gamma.png")

```

### Part II: Gamma

```{r 6.1, echo = F, out.width = "100%"}

knitr::include_graphics("U:/Thesis/programs/Simulation final/Gamma.png")

```

### Part II: Gamma

```{r 7, echo = F, out.width = "100%"}

knitr::include_graphics("U:/Thesis/programs/Simulation final/Gamma_Zoom.png")

```

### Part II: Summary

\begin{tabular}{l r r l r r }
\textbf{Method} & \multicolumn{4}{l}{\textbf{Parameter}} & \textbf{Bias (MSE)} \\ \hline
Uniform $\log(\sigma)$ & $a = $ & 2& &  & -0.0070 (0.00351)  \\
Uniform $\sigma$ & $a = $ & 100 & &  & -0.0047 (0.00371)  \\
Uniform $\sigma^2$ & $a = $ & 1000 & &  & 0.0003 (0.00357)  \\
Half-t & $df = $ & 100& s = & 500 & -0.0043 (0.00369)  \\
Gamma & $a = $ & 0.25& b = & 5 & 0.0004 (0.00335) 
\end{tabular}

\

* Uniform prior on $\sigma^2$ with $a = 1000$ and Gamma prior with $a = 0.25$ and $b = 5$ show the smallest bias.
* MSE is very similar for most parameter values and hyperpiros, except of extreme outliers.


# Next Steps

### Further Considerations

* Simulations will also be performed with smaller groups sizes, e.g. n = 20; depending on the results, further sample sizes will be investigated.
* Triple Bootstrap will be analysed for $B = 80$; depending on computation time, additional runs with $B = 100$ and $B = 200$ may be considered. 
* The maximal response in each dose-response relationship will be reduced to 60%, i.e. all responses will be scaled accordingly.

### Part III


Based on the findings from Part I/II ($B = 80$, Gamma prior on $\tau = \frac{1}{\sigma^2}$ ($a = 0.25$, $b = 5$) and Uniform on $\sigma^2$ with $a = 1000$), the following will be considered for the next comparison:

* Additional methods (Jackknife, Shrinkage Estimator, Hybrid estimators) from [@tianyu2024] will be investigated.
* For the Hybrid estimator [@tianyu2024], Double Bootstrap and the Shrinkage Estimator were combined. Based on Part I, Single Bootstrap and Shrinkage Estimator may be combined.
* Methods proposed by [@Stein] (additive and/or multiplicative adjustment) will be applied.
